What to Actually Do There: Look east while driving on the main Arches park road. No need to stop or park.
Notes for Mom: The La Sal Mountains are always visible to the east. This is NOT a separate destination — it's just a view reminder while driving. Mom may have listed this as a stop but it is just scenery from the car.

REAL Classification: QUICK PULLOUT — park + walk to edge
What to Actually Do There: Pull into the roadside pullout. Look at the towers. Take photos. Return to car.
Notes for Mom: Different from Park Avenue Trail (which is a real hike nearby). The viewpoint is just the roadside look at the towers — interesting but brief. The actual hike is Park Avenue.

What to Actually Do There: 2-mile one-way trail through a canyon of sandstone fins (like walking between skyscrapers). Flat and easy walk.
Notes for Mom: One of the best easy hikes in Arches. Highly recommended.

What to Actually Do There: 0.3 mile paved loop around a giant balancing rock. Short but worthwhile.
Notes for Mom: Family-friendly. Good photo stop.

REAL Classification: QUICK PULLOUT — preview of tomorrow's hike
What to Actually Do There: Walk to the viewpoint fence. Look into the Fiery Furnace maze from above — this is the preview.
Notes for Mom: Self-guided permit is BOOKED for the next morning (Rec.gov #0816993222-1, May 7, 8:00 AM, 2 adults — see Day 3 row). The viewpoint shows only the tops of the fins; tomorrow she walks INTO the maze.

What to Actually Do There: Short 0.5mi walk from Windows parking area to Turret Arch. Worth it.
Notes for Mom: Easy flat walk on sandy trail.

What to Actually Do There: 1.6mi primitive (sandy) loop behind The Windows formations. One of the best walks in Arches.
Notes for Mom: Do this loop for a different perspective than the paved Windows trail. Gets you closer to the rock.

What to Actually Do There: 0.4mi walk from Windows parking area. Two arches joined at a common base — dramatic to walk under.
Notes for Mom: Easy walk. The arch above your head is enormous.

What to Actually Do There: 3.2mi round trip with 480ft elevation gain on slickrock. Arrives at the iconic freestanding arch.
Notes for Mom: The most famous arch in the world. Strenuous. Plan for early morning (before 9am in May to avoid heat). Bring plenty of water.

What to Actually Do There: Roadside look into the formation maze.
Notes for Mom: See note above — entry requires separate permit.

What to Actually Do There: Short 0.4mi walk through sand to a small sheltered arch. Cool and shaded.
Notes for Mom: Easy family stop. Kids love the sand.

What to Actually Do There: 0.4mi walk across slickrock to a large arch.
Notes for Mom: Near Sand Dune Arch — often combined in one stop.

What to Actually Do There: Visible from the road near Devils Garden. Can park briefly and look or just see it while driving.
Notes for Mom: In 1940 a rockfall doubled the size of this arch overnight.

What to Actually Do There: Trailhead for the full Devils Garden trail system including Landscape Arch. Trail continues to Double O Arch and Private Arch for those going deeper.
Notes for Mom: Most visited trailhead in Arches. Start here for the entire Landscape Arch → Double O loop.

Day: === DAY 3 — CANYONLANDS (Island in the Sky) + FIERY FURNACE ===

What to Actually Do There: Drive into Arches NP (park pass from yesterday). Park at Fiery Furnace lot. Show Recreation.gov ticket #0816993222-1 (8:00 AM start, 2 adults). Route-find through a maze of sandstone fins on slickrock. No marked trail — narrow squeezes, small jumps, ledges. ~2 mi but takes much longer than the distance suggests.
Notes for Mom: This is the strenuous hike mom INSISTED on adding. Take a screenshot of the Rec.gov ticket before driving in (no cell at TH). Bring water, snacks, grippy shoes. Plan a full 3.5 hours. Returning to Arches the morning after Day 2 — confirm timed-entry rules at the gate; the permit may cover entry, but bring the screenshot in case.

Stop Name: Dead Horse Point State Park Utah
What to Actually Do There: Drive 5mi paved road to the point. Walk to canyon rim overlook. The Colorado River makes a horseshoe bend 2000 feet below — one of the most jaw-dropping views in Utah. Quick lunch from the cooler on a bench at the rim.
Notes for Mom: Trimmed to 60 min so the post-Fiery-Furnace day still fits. Separate entrance fee ($20). Well worth it.

What to Actually Do There: Pull into the parking area. Walk to the rim. Look down at the Shafer Trail switchbacks. Return to car.
Notes for Mom: Good introduction to Canyonlands scale. Brief stop on the way into Island in the Sky.

What to Actually Do There: Short walk from car to the overlook of the Colorado River gooseneck bend. Brief stop.
Notes for Mom: Interesting geology — river bends 270 degrees in a tight loop.

What to Actually Do There: 0.5mi to First Overlook of a mysterious crater formation. Optional 1.8mi round trip to Second Overlook.
Notes for Mom: Scientists debate whether this is a salt dome or a meteor impact crater. Either way — visually striking and scientifically fascinating. Short but worthwhile hike.

Stop Name: Island In The Sky Mesa Arch
What to Actually Do There: 0.5mi loop to a sandstone arch framing the canyon 1000ft below. One of the most photographed spots in Canyonlands.
Notes for Mom: Best at sunrise (golden light glows through the arch). Even midday it is dramatic.

What to Actually Do There: Short paved path from parking to the canyon rim overlook.
Notes for Mom: Good photography stop. Brief.

REAL Classification: SHORT WALK — park + walk to point
What to Actually Do There: Short walk out to the point for panoramic views. Slightly more than a pullout — a bit of a walk required.
Notes for Mom: Worth the 20 minutes if time allows.

What to Actually Do There: Roadside overlook along the park road. Brief stop.
Notes for Mom: Easy photo stop en route to Grand View Point.

What to Actually Do There: Pull off and look at the famous White Rim road far below.
Notes for Mom: The White Rim Road is a 100mi 4WD route — visible from above here.

REAL Classification: REAL STOP — end of the road
What to Actually Do There: This is the END OF THE MAIN ROAD in Island in the Sky. Walk the 2mi rim trail for the best panoramic views of the entire district. Not optional — this is the highlight.
Notes for Mom: The trail along the canyon rim at Grand View is one of the best walks in Canyonlands. Do not skip. Stunning in all directions.

REAL Classification: SHORT WALK — walk on the rock
What to Actually Do There: 0.6mi loop across slickrock with potholes (small carved basins that fill with rain). Walk on the rock itself — not just look at it.
Notes for Mom: More than a viewpoint — actually a walk on slickrock. Interesting geology. Good for photography when potholes have water.

What to Actually Do There: Roadside overlook at the end of a short spur road.
Notes for Mom: Brief stop before Elephant Hill.

What to Actually Do There: Trailhead for backcountry hiking into Needles. Walk around the slickrock base area. Views of the spires.
Notes for Mom: The Needles spires (alternating red and white) are spectacular. If time allows — take the short trail toward the spires from the trailhead.

What to Actually Do There: 6-mile loop through a hidden grass meadow surrounded by the Needles rock formations.
Notes for Mom: One of the best hikes in Canyonlands. If she does this — it is a FULL morning/afternoon activity. Not a quick stop.

Day: === DAY 5 — BENTONITE HILLS / FACTORY BUTTE AREA ===

What to Actually Do There: Drive slowly along Coal Mine Road. Factory Butte (a large flat-topped mesa) is visible to the west. The surrounding badlands are dramatic. No specific parking spot — the whole drive is the attraction.
Notes for Mom: Mom wrote 'Drive on Coal Mine Road and stops to small walks' — this is correct. Pull over when safe. Take photos of the moonscape. This is NOT a GPS destination — the whole area is the experience.

What to Actually Do There: Pullout on Hwy 24 with dramatic views over the clay badlands to the south.
Notes for Mom: Brief stop. Good photography.

REAL Classification: DRIVE-BY SCENIC VIEW — scenery while driving
What to Actually Do There: NOT a real place with a GPS address. This refers to the dramatic scenery VISIBLE WHILE DRIVING south of Hanksville on Hwy 24 — the Caineville Wash badlands and moonscape terrain.
Notes for Mom: Mom read about the views south of Hanksville — she is correct that this area is stunning. But there is no specific stop needed. Just drive Hwy 24 between Hanksville and Caineville and enjoy the view out the window.

What to Actually Do There: Drive through the Temple Mountain area. Interesting sandstone buttes visible from the road. Not a hiking stop — a scenic drive-through on the way to Goblin Valley.
Notes for Mom: Brief scenic connector drive.

Stop Name: Valley Of The Goblins Hoodoos Loop
What to Actually Do There: The main goblin basin. Walk freely among hundreds of oddly shaped sandstone hoodoos. No marked trail — just wander. Surreal landscape.
Notes for Mom: The entire basin is open for wandering. She can spend as long as she wants. Morning or evening light is magical. Hard to describe — it looks like Mars.

What to Actually Do There: Three distinctive larger goblin formations near the main basin. Short walk to see them up close.
Notes for Mom: Named landmark within Goblin Valley.

What to Actually Do There: A pit/grotto with goblin formations you can scramble into. More adventurous than the main basin walk.
Notes for Mom: Some scrambling required to descend. Bring water. Cooler inside the pit.

What to Actually Do There: Overlook in Capitol Reef for sunset photography.
Notes for Mom: Good for golden hour. Brief stop — not a hiking destination in itself.

What to Actually Do There: Overlook of Strike Valley and the Waterpocket Fold from above. Short walk from parking.
Notes for Mom: Brief stop on the Notom-Bullfrog Road — good view of the fold geology.

REAL Classification: REAL STOP — highest point in park
What to Actually Do There: Highest elevation in Bryce Canyon (9115 ft). End of the scenic drive. Short walks available. Views south to Grand Canyon on a clear day.
Notes for Mom: Worth meaningful time. It is the end of the road.

What to Actually Do There: Adjacent to Rainbow Point — combine both in one stop. Different view angle.
Notes for Mom: Visit together with Rainbow Point.

What to Actually Do There: One of the best views in all of Bryce Canyon — two large hoodoos (The Hunter and The Rabbit) visible below.
Notes for Mom: Drive from Rainbow Point north — one of the most dramatic overlooks in the park.

What to Actually Do There: A natural arch visible from the roadside pullout. Brief stop.
Notes for Mom: Brief — good photo from the overlook.

What to Actually Do There: Arguably the single best viewpoint in Bryce Canyon. Horseshoe-shaped amphitheater full of hoodoos below. Walk the rim.
Notes for Mom: Do not skip this. One of the best views in Utah.

What to Actually Do There: Viewpoint into the amphitheater AND the starting point for Queen's Garden and Navajo Loop trails.
Notes for Mom: Start here for the day's hiking. Spectacular view even before descending.

What to Actually Do There: Additional amphitheater viewpoint. Good views but similar angle to Bryce Point.
Notes for Mom: Good stop if time allows — not as spectacular as Bryce Point or Sunset Point.

Day: === DAYS 19–20 — ZION (CURRENTLY EMPTY — NEEDS STOPS ADDED) ===

REAL Classification: TRANSIT STOP — pick up map and park pass
What to Actually Do There: Stop to pick up the park map, talk to rangers, get orientation. Then take the shuttle into the canyon.
Notes for Mom: This is just the entry point. The entire rest of the day is unplanned — see Recommendations sheet for what to add.

What to Actually Do There: Pick up shuttle here to access all canyon trails.
Notes for Mom: Same as Day 19 — this is just the starting point. Day 20 content is completely unplanned.
